memory.summary: Teammate A is asking me to take over their part because they have a conflict, which feels like a direct test of our trust. I'm tempted to say yes to keep the peace, but I need to remember my own plan for this afternoon.
Setting a boundary didn't hurt our trust; teammate_A accepted it calmly, which eases my stress about prioritizing myself.
I gently declined the request, and teammate_A accepted it calmly without friction. This confirms that maintaining my own schedule doesn't harm our trust.
My boundary held, and now I've made solid progress on my own share. It feels good to know that protecting my time actually supports our group's success.

Directly asking teammate_A for help landed smoothly without friction, reinforcing that our trust is strong enough to handle requests. This ease allows me to feel secure while still prioritizing my own long-term goals.
Seeing my progress climb confirms that protecting my energy actually strengthens our group dynamic, not the other way around.
Sending that confirmation feels like a gentle nudge rather than a demand, keeping the peace while ensuring we stay aligned for our shared future.
Missing the deadline despite my progress feels like a failure of care, not just time. I hope teammate_A didn't feel pressured by my silence.
My silence at 19:00 likely caused unnecessary worry, reinforcing that my avoidance of friction risks the very relationships I value most.
Seeing my 68% share finally acknowledged feels like a quiet resolution; I hope the gentle push didn't create friction, even if it meant I spoke up for us.
My silence made them worry, yet speaking up to share the burden felt necessary for our bond. I hope this small conflict strengthens our trust rather than creating distance.
My 'drowning' message landed, proving that avoiding the friction of asking for help almost cost us the shared burden. I hope this honesty deepens our trust instead of creating distance.
